title: Quiz: Quantum Kernel Applications
description: Test your understanding of quantum kernel applications

questions.question: In a Quantum SVM (QSVM), which part runs on a quantum computer?
questions.explanation: In QSVM, the quantum computer is used to compute the kernel matrix K(x_i, x_j) by preparing quantum states and measuring overlaps. The actual SVM optimization (finding support vectors, computing weights) is done classically.

questions.question: What is kernel ridge regression used for?
questions.explanation: Kernel ridge regression is a regression method that predicts continuous target values. It uses the kernel trick to fit non-linear functions while maintaining the computational benefits of linear regression through regularization.

questions.question: In one-class SVM for anomaly detection, what does the model learn?

options.text: A description of normal data to identify outliers

questions.explanation: One-class SVM learns a boundary around the normal training data. Points that fall outside this boundary are classified as anomalies. It's trained only on normal examples and identifies outliers as points that deviate from the learned distribution.

questions.question: Quantum kernels always outperform classical kernels on all machine learning tasks.
questions.explanation: False. Quantum kernels are not universally better. Their advantage depends on the specific data structure, the choice of feature map, hardware noise levels, and whether the data has structure that quantum circuits can exploit. Classical kernels often perform comparably or better on many standard datasets.

questions.question: Which preprocessing step is most important for quantum kernel methods?

options.text: Scaling data to an appropriate range (e.g., [0, 2π])

questions.explanation: Scaling data to an appropriate range (typically [0, 2π] or similar) is crucial for quantum kernels because the data values become rotation angles in the quantum circuit. If values are too large or small, the quantum feature map may not effectively distinguish between data points.
